did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a 207-combination machine learning computational framework to distinguish basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and actinic keratosis (AK) from dermoscopic images. Specifically, it employed a ResNet50 deep learning model for feature extraction via transfer learning, applied Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) for visualization, and integrated 15 machine learning algorithms (including XGBoost and Lasso regression) to build a predictive diagnostic model.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document explicitly labels itself as a 'Research Paper' in the header. The study presents original experimental work involving data collection from the HAM10000 and DAYISET 1 datasets, development of a deep learning model (ResNet50) and machine learning framework (207 combinations), and validation of a diagnostic model for distinguishing basal cell carcinoma and actinic keratosis.

title: Dermoscopy-based Radiomics Help Distinguish Basal Cell Carcinoma and Actinic Keratosis: A Large-scale Real-world Study Based on a 207-combination Machine Learning Computational Framework
brief_summary: This study developed a machine learning-based predictive model to differentiate between basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and actinic keratosis (AK) using dermoscopic images. The researchers compiled 904 images from the public HAM10000 dataset and a proprietary dataset (DAYISET 1) from the First Affiliated Hospital of Dalian Medical University. A ResNet50 deep learning model was employed for feature extraction via transfer learning, followed by the integration of 15 machine learning algorithms to generate 207 combinations. The final diagnostic model, combining XGBoost with Lasso regression, achieved an AUC of 1.000 in the training set and an average AUC of 0.695 across three validation sets. The authors conclude that this discriminative model demonstrates excellent predictive capabilities and could enhance clinical decision-making efficiency while reducing unnecessary biopsies.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task. Included 514 cases from HAM10000 and 32 cases from DAYISET 1.
One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task. Included 327 cases from HAM10000 and 31 cases from DAYISET 1.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and actinic keratosis (AK)
all_task_types.details: The study developed a predictive model for differentiating between dermoscopic images of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and actinic keratosis (AK). It utilized a 207-combination machine learning framework including XGBoost and Lasso regression to perform binary classification. Performance was evaluated using metrics such as AUC, accuracy, F-score, and recall, with confusion matrices used to assess diagnostic outcomes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly accessible dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) database, used for skin disease diagnosis and research. Images were curated from this dataset for the study.

use.training: 50% of the curated HAM10000 images (n=422) were allocated as the training set (Dataset A).
use.testing: The remaining 50% of the curated HAM10000 images (n=419) served as internal test set 1 (Dataset B).
use.other: The HAM10000 dataset provided the majority of the data for the internal test set 2 (Dataset C) along with the training set components, totaling 841 images used in the internal validation framework.

datasets_used.name: DAYISET 1
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of dermoscopic images collected from the First Affiliated Hospital of Dalian Medical University between February 2021 and October 2023. Images were acquired using a FotoFinder dermoscope.

use.testing: Used as an external validation set (Dataset D) to validate the model's accuracy and enhance clinical applicability.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To perform differential analysis on deep learning features and select the top 30 differentially expressed features with the smallest p-values.
To determine statistical significance for differential analysis; p-values less than 0.05 were considered significant.
Used in conjunction with p-value for differential analysis significance (set at 1).
To perform random grouping (data segmentation) and cross-validation for model training and evaluation.
To train the consensus model (XGBoost combined with Lasso regression) and evaluate its performance.
To quantify model classification accuracy and performance by calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC).
To evaluate predictive performance by comparing actual diagnostic outcomes against model predictions.
To visualize and assess the calibration of the predictive model across different datasets.
To visualize the results of the differential analysis.
To perform statistical analysis of baseline demographic and clinical data.

hypothesis_testing.test: Limma package differential analysis (for feature selection) and statistical analysis of baseline demographic/clinical data (likely t-tests or chi-square tests via SPSS, though specific test names for demographics are not explicitly stated, p-values are reported in Table 1).
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: For feature selection: No difference in deep learning features between BCC and AK groups. For demographics: No difference in demographic/clinical characteristics (age, gender, location) between BCC and AK groups within each dataset.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: For feature selection: Significant difference in deep learning features between BCC and AK groups. For demographics: Significant difference in demographic/clinical characteristics between BCC and AK groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: P-values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant. For feature selection, the top 30 features with the smallest p-values were selected. For demographics, p-values were calculated to compare baseline characteristics between BCC and AK cohorts (e.g., age p=0.742/0.937, gender p=0.082/0.877, location p<0.001/0.234).

Dataset_A_Training_Set.notes: The model achieved perfect values for accuracy, F-score, and recall. The calibration curve perfectly overlapped with the ideal calibration curve.

Dataset_B_Internal_Test_Set_1.notes: Referred to as DATASET 2 in results section 3.4.

Dataset_C_Internal_Test_Set_2.notes: Referred to as DATASET 3 in results section 3.4.

Dataset_D_External_Validation_Set.notes: Referred to as DATASET 4 in results section 3.4.

Average_Validation_Sets.notes: The average AUC across the three validation sets (Datasets B, C, and D) was 0.695.

Mean_Performance_Metrics_Validation_Sets.notes: Mean values calculated across the three validation sets.

Sensitivity_BCC_Diagnosis.notes: Sensitivity for diagnosing BCC in different datasets.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning techniques applied; model pre-trained on images and fine-tuned based on optimal model output from pre-training results.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Deep-learning features with dimensions of 2048 extracted through the average pooling layer.
architecture_details.feature_normalization: Feature values normalized to the range of 0–1.
architecture_details.interpretability_method: Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) used for visualization.

training_details.framework: 207-combination machine learning computational framework integrating 15 classical algorithms (including neural networks, logistic regression, LDA, QDA, KNN, decision tree, random forest, XGBoost, ridge regression, LASSO, elastic net, SVM, GBM, stepwise logistic regression, and naive Bayes).
training_details.final_model: Consensus model formed by integrating XGBoost with Lasso regression.

classification_or_output_head.evaluation_metrics: AUC, Accuracy, F-score, Recall, Sensitivity, Specificity.
classification_or_output_head.visualization: ROC curves, Calibration curves, Confusion matrices.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO)
Consensus Model (XGBoost combined with Lasso regression)
preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of deep-learning feature values to the range of 0–1
Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) for visualization of input image regions
conclusions: The study developed a discriminative diagnostic model based on a 207-combination machine learning computational framework that effectively differentiates between basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and actinic keratosis (AK) using dermoscopic images.

The final predictive model, formed by integrating XGBoost with Lasso regression, exhibited excellent predictive capabilities, achieving an AUC of 1.000 in the training set and an average AUC of 0.695 across three validation sets.
The model demonstrated high sensitivity in the training set and maintained stable performance across internal and external validation sets.
The constructed model has the potential to enhance clinical decision-making efficiency, reduce unnecessary biopsies, and provide valuable guidance for further treatment.
important_remarks: The model achieved perfect performance (AUC=1.000, Accuracy=1.000) in the training set, which indicates potential overfitting or data leakage, especially given the significant drop in performance in validation sets (average AUC=0.695).
The study is retrospective, and the authors explicitly note the need for prospective studies to validate the stability of the diagnostic model.
The classification model cannot predict subtypes for BCC and AK due to the absence of subtype annotations in the original data.
The external validation set (DAYISET 1) is very small (n=63), which limits the robustness of the external validation results.
The study relies on a proprietary dataset from a single institution (First Affiliated Hospital of Dalian Medical University) for external validation, which may introduce population or dataset bias.

The model was developed using a 207-combination machine learning framework, but the final consensus model (XGBoost + Lasso) performance in validation sets (AUC ~0.63-0.82) suggests moderate rather than excellent generalizability compared to the training set.
